risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We used a computerized block randomization procedure (allocation ratio 1:1, block size 10)." Comment: This explicitly describes a computerized randomization method with block randomization, which indicates a proper random allocation sequence was generated. The additional statement that the randomization was conducted by a researcher with no involvement in enrollment/assignment further supports adequate sequence generation, but the key evidence is the computerized block randomization procedure.

Quote: "We used a computerized block randomization procedure (allocation ratio 1:1, block size 10). The researcher conducting the randomization had no information about the participants apart from their 6-digit codes and did not participate in the enrollment and assignment of the participants to study groups, which was handled by two different researchers." Comment: Although a computerized procedure is sequence generation, the paper explicitly states that the person generating the allocation had no identifying participant information beyond a code and was separate from enrollment/assignment, which indicates the recruiter could not foresee the upcoming assignment before enrolment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a two-armed open-label randomized controlled trial." Comment: The study explicitly states it was open-label, so participants and personnel were not blinded to allocation. Because the main outcomes included self-assessed depressive symptoms (PHQ-9, BDI-II) and a satisfaction measure, knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence reporting and behavior, making performance bias likely.

Quote: "The primary outcomes were self-assessed depressive symptoms with the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) and the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI II)." "Information on work absenteeism was retrieved from health insurance records." Comment: The outcome assessor was the participant for PHQ-9/BDI-II self-reports, and the data source for sickness absence was routine insurance records. Although the trial was open-label, the self-reported depression outcomes are susceptible to bias from lack of participant blinding; however, the review context here includes an objective outcome (sickness absence) measured from insurance records, which is unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Because the assessor for the objective outcome was effectively the record system/abstracted records and measurement could not be meaningfully biased, detection bias is low for this domain overall.

Quote: "Of the 180 participants, 88 completed the post-assessment after 12 weeks (retention rate: 48.8%, 88/180), and 58 completed the follow-up assessment after 24 weeks (retention rate: 32.2%, 58/180)." Comment: Attrition was very large overall, with just under half completing post-assessment and only about one-third completing follow-up. Although the authors used ITT with multiple imputation for post-treatment depression outcomes, the paper also reports that follow-up completion was low and that sickness absence data were unavailable for 20 participants without imputation. This level of missing data could plausibly bias the results, so the domain is high risk.
